IA No. 163371/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 27-05-2026 These matters were called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Shyam Diwan, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Vijay Hansaria, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Ritesh Agrawal, AOR
                   Mr. Sohel Rishabh, Adv.
                   Ms. Priyanshi Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Ankur Singhal, Adv.
                   Ms. Nandini Rai, Adv.
                   Mr. Aashay Shukla, Adv.
                   Mr. Jitendra Mahapatra, Adv.
                   
1

--- Page 107 ---
                 
For Respondent(s) : 
                   Mr. Navin Pahwa, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Mohit D. Ram, AOR
                   Ms. Nayan Gupta, Adv.
                   Mr. Rafid Akhter, Adv.
                   
                 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Application for amendment in cause title is allowed
in SLP (Civil) No.18769 of 2026.
2. Since  the  issues  raised  in  both  the  captioned
petitions are the same and the parties are also the same,
those were taken up for hearing analogously and are being
disposed of by this common order.
3. It appears from the materials on record that the
Porbandar Municipal Corporation (for short, “the PMC”)
issued an invitation for bids dated 15th January, 2026 for
the  work of  designing and  construction of  a Sewage
Treatment Plant of 11.00 MLD and 3.00 MLD respectively
and various pumping stations along with all allied works.
The  estimated  contract  valuation  was  approximately
Rs.122.97 crore.  The last date to offer the bids was
fixed as 4 th February, 2026.  Later the said date was
extended to 23 rd February, 2026.  The bids were to be
submitted on or before 23 rd February, 2026 online by 5.30
p.m.  
2

--- Page 108 ---
4. It is the case of the petitioner that it offered
its  bid  online.   Along  with  the  petitioner,  the
respondent  No.1, namely,  M/s. R  & B  Infra Projects
Limited also offered its bid.  Technical and financial
evaluation took place of the bids.  It is not in dispute
that the work order came to be issued in favour of the
petitioner on 30 th March, 2026.  The respondent No.1
before us went before the High Court by way of a Writ
Petition being Special Civil Application No.4641 of 2026
being aggrieved and dissatisfied with the communication
received  from  the  tendering  authority.   This
communication was in the form of an intimation that the
respondent No.1 was not eligible to offer its bid.  
5. The claim of the respondent No.1 was rejected on
the ground that it had failed to furnish the registration
certificate of the other State,  in the sense that it was
not uploaded and no application was made to the Road and
Building department for AA class registration before the
tender submission.  The High Court took the view that
since  the respondent  No.1 was  unable to  upload the
necessary documents online and as one of the clauses of
the  tender  documents,  more  particularly,  Clause  24
therein permitted the respondent No.1 to provide physical
copies of the documents, the High Court allowed the
petition and directed the Corporation to consider the
documents which the respondent No.1 would physically
3

--- Page 109 ---
submit.  This order passed by the High Court dated 1 st
April, 2026 is also a subject matter of challenge before
us at the instance of the petitioner.  
6. Insofar as the impugned order dated 8th May, 2026 is
concerned, two petitions came to be disposed of by the
High Court.  Special Civil Application No.5436 of 2026
was preferred by the respondent No.1 before us whereas
Special Civil Application No.4647 of 2026 was preferred
by a third party, namely, Yogi Construction Co.  In the
Writ Petition i.e. Special Civil Application No.5436 of
2026 is concerned, it was pointed out by the respondent
No.1 before the High Court, as original petitioner, that
the petitioner before us, namely, SNL & Company is not
eligible to participate in the tender because it had no
work experience.  The High Court adjudicated both the
Writ Petitions referred to above and by a common order
allowed the Writ Petition preferred by the respondent
No.1 and rejected the Writ Petition which was filed by a
third party i.e. Yogi Construction Co.  While allowing
the  Writ  Petition  preferred  by  the  respondent  No.1
insofar as the work experience of the present petitioner
is concerned, the High Court observed as under:
“[58]. The reason that we are constrained to
make these observations regarding the conduct of
the Corporation is because, it is an admitted
fact in the present case that N.P.Patel and
Company,  the  partnership  firm  to  which  Mr.
Siddharth Narendrakumar unagariya, the proprietor
4

--- Page 110 ---
of  SNL  &  Company  is  a  partner,  has  been
blacklisted in respect of other tenders, and this
blacklisting has also been upheld by the Courts.
As a result of this blacklisting, N.P.Patel and
Company  would  have  been  disqualified  from
participating in the present tender.  In order to
get over this disqualification, on NP Patel & Co,
its major partner i.e., the proprietor of SNL &
Company has submitted its bid as a proprietary
concern.”
7. To put it in other words, two things weighed with
the High Court in taking the view that the petitioner is
not eligible.  First, the petitioner is a proprietary
concern of which he is the proprietor himself and insofar
as  the  work  experience  is  concerned,  the  petitioner
relied upon the work experience of a partnership firm in
which he is one of the partners holding 90% share.  The
High Court also took into consideration the fact that the
partnership firm, namely, N.P.Patel & Company in which
the petitioner is one of the partners having 90% share
was blacklisted.
8. In  such  circumstances  referred  to  above,  the
petitioner is here before us with the two petitions.
9. We heard Mr. Shyam Divan and Mr. Vijay Hansaria,
the learned senior counsel appearing for the petitioner
and Mr. Navin Pahwa, the learned senior counsel with Mr.
Mohit  D.Ram,  the  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the
respondent No.1.
5

--- Page 111 ---
10. Without  entering  into  the  merits  and  without
observing  anything  further  and  having  regard  to  the
materials on record and the submissions canvassed, we are
of the view that the PMC should go for a fresh tender
with respect to the work which it wants to undertake.
Insofar as the tender notice dated 15 th January, 2026 is
concerned, we cancel it. 
11. The PMC shall now proceed to issue a fresh tender
in  accordance  with  law.  If  the  petitioner  and  the
respondent  No.1  want  to  participate  in  fresh  tender
notice and if they are otherwise eligible, they may offer
their bids in accordance with the terms of the tender
notice.
12. With the aforesaid, the Special Leave Petitions are
disposed of.
13. Pending applications shall also stand disposed of.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                         (POOJA SHARMA)
   AR-CUM-PS                              COURT MASTER
6

--- Page 112 ---
1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL No.        OF 2026
[arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 4407 OF 2026]
AASHISH VASANT MORE                         APPELLANT
                           VERSUS
THE STATE OF MAHARASHTRA                   RESPONDENT
 
O R D E R 
1.   Leave granted.
2. By  the  impugned  judgment  and  order  dated  22 nd January,
2026,  the  High  Court  of  Judicature  at  Bombay  has  rejected  the
appellant’s prayer for bail in anticipation of arrest.  
3. Appellant figures as an accused in FIR No. 265 dated 16 th July,
2025 registered with Police Station Manikpur, District Palghar.  It is
alleged  in  the  FIR  that  the  appellant  has  committed  offence(s)
punishable under Section(s) 318(4), 316(5), 3(5) of the Bharatiya
Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023 and Sections 3 and 4 of the Maharashtra
Protection of Interest of Depositors (in Financial Establishments) Act,
1999.
4. We have heard learned counsel appearing for the appellant as

--- Page 113 ---
2
well as the respondent.
5. It is not in dispute that the appellant, in terms of an earlier
order dated 16th March, 2026, has joined the investigation. However,
the investigation is yet to conclude qua the appellant.
6. T aking an overall view of the matter including consideration of
the nature of allegations levelled against the appellant, we are of the
considered opinion that his custodial interrogation is not necessary
and that there is sufficient ground for him to be admitted to an order
for grant of bail in anticipation of arrest.
7. Accordingly,  the  impugned  judgment  and  order  stands  set
aside.
8. It is directed that in the event of the appellant being arrested
in connection with proceedings arising out of the aforesaid FIR, he
shall be released on bail by the arresting/investigating officer/trial
court on terms and conditions to be fixed by the trial court.
9. It is made clear that in the event the appellant breaches any of
the terms and conditions imposed by it, the trial court shall be at
liberty to cancel the bail of the appellant.
10. Needless  to  observe,  the  appellant  shall  not,  directly  or
indirectly, by making inducement, threat or promise, dissuade any
person acquainted with the facts of the case from disclosing such
facts to any police officer or to the court.
11. Since the investigation is yet to conclude qua the appellant,

--- Page 114 ---
3
the investigating officer will be free to require the appellant to face
further interrogation. In such an event, the appellant shall respond to
any notice  that might be  issued in  this  regard  failing  which  this
omission shall be considered as misuse of the bail conditions and the
prosecution will be at liberty to seek cancellation of bail by applying
before the trial court. 
12. We clarify that the observations made in this order and grant of
bail will not be treated as findings on the merits of the case.
13. The appeal is allowed on the above terms.
14.  Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
………………..……………………...J.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
   CIVIL APPEAL  NO.            OF 2026   
(@ Special Leave Petition (C)  No. 929/2025)
RANGANATH K.L.               Appellant
VERSUS
SHOBHA GODABOLE @ SHOBHA SAHASRABUDDE & ORS.        Respondent
O R D E R
Leave granted.
2. Being aggrieved by the judgment dated 02.12.2024 passed by
the High Court of Karnataka in Regular Second Appeal No.2118 of
2023, the defendant in O.S. No. 274 of 2007 has preferred this
appeal.
3. Learned  counsel  for  the  appellant  and  learned  senior
counsel  for  contesting  respondent  no.1/original  plaintiff
submitted in unison that the matter was referred to the Supreme
Court  Mediation  Centre  to  explore  the  possibility  of  a
settlement between the parties; that by Settlement Agreement
dated 25.05.2026, the parties have resolved to settle their
dispute which arises from the final decree proceedings of the
Senior Civil Judge and JMFC, Soraba pursuant to a preliminary
1

--- Page 123 ---
decree of partition made in O.S. No. 274 of 2007 and thereafter
the matter having travelled in Regular Appeal No. 224 of 2011
and subsequently in RSA No. 374 of 2017  and connected appeals
and FDP No. 5 of 2017. The final decree was assailed in RA No.
10016 of 2022 and later in RFA No. 2118 of 2023.
4. Learned counsel for the respective parties submitted that
the parties have agreed to the terms of the settlement and
therefore, this appeal may be disposed of on the basis of the
settlement agreement arrived at between the parties. They also
stated  that  the  original  settlement  agreement  has  been
couriered and would be placed on the file of this Court. Hence,
the true copy of the settlement agreement which has been signed
by the respective parties and the learned counsel may be taken
on record and the appeal may be disposed of.
5. The appellant and contesting respondent No. 1 have appeared
through  video  conferencing  facility  and  they  have  been
identified by their respective counsel.
6. When queried, they stated that they have indeed arrived at
a settlement of their dispute and they are agreeable to the
terms  and  conditions  of  the  settlement  agreement  dated
25.05.2026; that the settlement has been arrived at on their
own free volition and without there being any coercion or undue
influence from any side;  that the parties would abide by the
terms of the settlement agreement.
2

--- Page 124 ---
7. In the above backdrop, we have taken on record  the  copy
of Settlement Agreement dated 25.05.2026 pending receipt of the
original settlement agreement.
8. We have perused the terms of the Settlement Agreement. On
perusal of the same,  we find that the terms are lawful and
there is no legal impediment in accepting the same.
9. The dispute between the parties has been amicably settled.
The terms of  settlement agreement dated 25.05.2026 shall be
read as a  part and parcel of this order.
10. Consequently, the impugned judgment dated 02.12.2024 is
substituted by this settlement. The judgment passed in R.A.
No.10016 of 2022 by the V th Additional District and Sessions
Judge, Shivamogga and final decree dated 20.07.2022 passed in
F.D.P No. 5/2017 by the Senior Civil Judge and JMFC, Soraba
stands  modified.
11. As and when the original settlement agreement is received
by the  Supreme Court Mediation Centre, the same shall be
placed on the file of this Court.
12. The litigation expenses of Rs.25,000/- which was deposited
by the petitioner before the Registry of this Court shall be
made over to respondent no.1 along with the accrued interest,
if any.
13. The appeal is disposed of in the aforesaid terms.
3

--- Page 125 ---
14. We are informed that the learned mediator has endeavoured
to bring about a settlement between the parties. We place on
record our sincere appreciation of the efforts and endeavours
of the learned mediator. 
Pending application(s) shall stand disposed of.

  [B.V. NAGARATHNA]
 ….……………………………………J.

The appellant seeks anticipatory bail in
connection  with  FIR  No.994/2025,  dated
26.12.2025, registered at Police Station -
Karnal  Sadar,  District  –  Karnal,  for  the
offences punishable under Sections 316(2) and
318(4) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023

--- Page 128 ---
2
and  Section  24  of  the  Immigration  and
Foreigners Act, 2025.
We  have  heard  the  learned  counsel
appearing for the parties.
We have been informed that pursuant to
our order dated 24.04.2026, the appellant has
joined the investigation. The fact remains
that there are no prior criminal antecedents
in which the appellant is said to be involved.
Considering the above, we are inclined to
grant anticipatory bail to the appellant.
Accordingly, the impugned order stands
set  aside  and  the  appellant  is  granted
anticipatory bail on terms and conditions to
the satisfaction of the concerned Trial Court.
We make it clear that the appellant shall
continue to cooperate with the investigation
till its conclusion.

Leave granted.
The  appellant  seeks  regular  bail  in
connection with FIR No.49/2022, dated 09.03.2022,
registered at Police Station Umadi, District –
Sangli, for the offences punishable under Sections
25 and 4 of the Arms Act, 1959 and Sections 142,
147, 148, 149, 302, 307, 341 and 506 of the Indian
Penal  Code,  1860  and  Section  135  of  the
Maharashtra Police Act, 1951.

--- Page 133 ---
2
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
Notwithstanding the fact that the charges
have been framed way back in the year 2024, no
progress has been made even for initiating the
examination of any of the witnesses before the
Trial Court as of now. The appellant has been
under incarceration for a period of four years and
two months.
Suffice it is to state that the continued
incarceration  of  the  appellant  is  no  longer
required.
In such view of the matter, we are inclined
to grant bail to the appellant.
Accordingly, the impugned order stands set
aside and the appellant is granted bail on the
terms and conditions subject to the satisfaction
of the concerned Trial Court.

--- Page 134 ---
3
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       /2026
  [@ SLP [CRL.] NO.4839/2026]
KHAGE NEPARNE @ KHADAK SINGH     Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF HIMACHAL PRADESH        Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The appellant seeks bail in connection with
FIR No.136/2021, dated 20.08.2021 registered at
Police Station: Bhuntar, District – Kullu, for the
offences punishable under Sections 452 and 302 of
the Indian Penal Code, 1860.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
The case against the appellant is that he has
committed the alleged offence by using an iron
rod.
Learned counsel appearing for the appellant
submitted that many witnesses have turned hostile,
and the informant is not cooperating with the

--- Page 138 ---
2
trial. The  appellant  has  been  under
incarceration for a period of five years and nine
months, and only 16 out of 29 witnesses have been
examined so far.  
Learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  State,
while opposing the appeal, submitted that the
appellant is a Nepali citizen, and he might flea.
Considering the period of incarceration, and
the facts as narrated above, we are inclined to
grant bail to the appellant.
Accordingly, the impugned order is set aside,
and the appellant is granted bail on terms and
conditions to the satisfaction of the concerned
Trial Court, with the observation that among other
conditions that can be imposed by the Trial Court,
the Trial Court shall impose a condition that the
appellant shall not leave the jurisdiction of the
Trial Court. 

--- Page 139 ---
3
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.
........................J.
[M.M. SUNDRESH]
........................J.
[NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH]
NEW DELHI;
MAY 29, 2026.

CONTEMPT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 592 OF 2025
IN

ASIAMBI SHAIKH & ANR.                           PETITIONER(S)

P2 MS. NUTAN KALE

NEETAL P. AMONKAR, MANAGING DIRECTOR,
GOA HOUSING BOARD                                RESPONDENT(S)/
 ALLEGED CONTEMNOR
O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the parties.
2. The order, of which contempt is alleged, had
directed the respondent to allow the petitioners
and similarly situated persons, if any, to take
part in the next ensuing examination and if they
succeeded in that examination their promotion which
had been withdrawn, shall stand confirmed failing
which reversion shall follow from the date of
failure. It was further directed that since the
1

--- Page 143 ---
CONTEMPT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 592 OF 2025
petitioner  no.1  was  due  to  superannuate  on
30.04.2025, it was directed that if before that
date no examination was held, the petitioner no.1
shall be deemed to be promoted to the post in
question.
3. Pursuant to the filing of the present contempt
petition,  in  the  Interlocutory  Application
No.102670 of 2026 filed on behalf of the respondent
i.e., by the Managing Director, Goa Housing Board
the stand is taken that the promotion has been
granted to the petitioner no.1 from 06.05.2022 to
30.04.2025  i.e.,  up  to  the  date  of  his
superannuation, as the examination after the order
of this Court dated 12.02.2025, was held in May,
2025. With regard to the petitioner no.2, the stand
is that she has been given the promotion from
06.05.2022 to the date of declaration of the result
by the Directorate of Accounts i.e., on 30.06.2025,
since she has not appeared in the examination held
in May, 2025. However, it is further stated that a
decision has been taken to create two supernumerary
2

--- Page 144 ---
CONTEMPT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 592 OF 2025
posts of Accountant of Group C Level in order to
accommodate the above two persons. Moreover, it has
also been brought on record that the Goa Housing
Board  has  further  deliberated  that  one  more
opportunity be given to the petitioner no.2 to
appear for the forthcoming Accountants examination
under intimation to the Government. We further note
that  in  the  said  Interlocutory  Application,
affirmed on 02.04.2026, it is stated that the final
examination  for  Accountants  has  not  yet  been
scheduled by the Directorate and the same shall be
communicated once it is finalized. The current
position with regard to the petitioner no.2 is that
she has been reverted to the post of Accountant
from that of UDC w.e.f. 01.07.2025.
4. The aforesaid stand is taken note of and the
respondent is held bound  by the said statement.
5. In the background what has been stated above,
we find that the order, of which Contempt has been
alleged, has been complied with.
6. Accordingly,  the  present  contempt  petition
3

--- Page 145 ---
CONTEMPT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 592 OF 2025
stands  disposed  of  along  with  all  pending
application(s).

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.          OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 5522 of 2026)
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH                 Appellant (s)
VERSUS
AMAN SINGH                            Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1) Leave granted. 
2) The State has preferred this appeal assailing
order dated 05.08.2025 passed by the High Court 1
allowing MCRCA No. 919 of 2025 preferred by the
respondent herein and granting him anticipatory bail
in relation to summons issued under Section 70 of
the Chhattisgarh State Goods and Services Tax Act,
2017(hereinafter referred to as ‘CGST Act’), by the
Office  of  the  Commissioner  of  State  Tax,
1  High Court of Chhattisgarh at Bilaspur
1

--- Page 148 ---
Crl. A. No.       / 2026 (@SLP (Crl.) No. 5522/ 2026)
Chhattisgarh,  for  the  offence  punishable  under
Section 132 of the CGST Act. 
3) We  find  that  the  allegation  of  suspicious
transactions with 13 high-risk suppliers, including
availing  fraudulent  Input  Tax  Credit  of  Rs.
11,08,79,628/- without actual supply any goods, has
been alleged in the present case, to which entries
would be relevant along with records of books of
accounts.
4) During  the  course  of  the  hearing,  it  was
informed that the respondent is already in custody
in another case. 
5) After hearing learned counsel for the parties
at  length  and  in  the  peculiar  facts  and
circumstances of this case, in our view, granting of
pre-arrest bail for the reasons as stated in the
impugned order by the High Court is not justified.
Accordingly,  the  impugned  order  granting
anticipatory bail stands set aside.  
6) The respondent be taken into custody in present
case  as  well,  and  subjected  to  custodial
interrogation, as contended, for the purpose of
2

--- Page 149 ---
Crl. A. No.       / 2026 (@SLP (Crl.) No. 5522/ 2026)
ascertaining  the  truth  and  to  supply  of  the
document.  
7) We make it clear that the setting aside of the
order of anticipatory bail would not come in the way
in case the respondent applies for grant of regular
bail.
8) The criminal appeal is, accordingly, allowed.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.)  No(s).  8487/2026
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated  17-04-2026 in

Allahabad]
MAHIPAL SINGH                                      Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH & ANR.                      Respondent(s)
FOR ADMISSION, IA No. 130084/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T. 
 
Date : 29-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Zulfiqar Ali Khan, AOR
                   Mr. Mohd Parvez Khan, Adv.
                   Mr. M Dhandapani, Adv.
                   Mr. Syed Saifullah, Adv.                   
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Rakshit Rathi, Adv.
                   Mr. Mandeep Baisla, Adv.
                   Mr. Vikas Bidhuri, Adv.
                   Mr. Ananya Singhal, Adv.
                   Ms. Tanya Verma, Adv.
                   Mr. Krishna Kumar, Adv.
                   Dr. Mrs. Vipin Gupta, AOR
                   Ms. Nandani Gupta, Adv.                   
                   
                   Mr. Vishwa Pal Singh, AOR
                   Mr. Adesh Kumar Gill, Adv.
                   Mr. Srajan Shankar Kulshrestha, Adv.
                   Mr. Abhijit Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Neha Gupta, Adv.
                   Mr. Babangir Uttamgir Gosawi, Adv.     
Mr. R.Kulshreshta, Adv.*              
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. Zulfiqar Ali Khan, learned counsel for the
petitioner, Mr. R.Kulshreshtha, learned counsel for the State

--- Page 156 ---
2
and  Mr. Rakshit Rathi, learned counsel for respondent no.2.
2. By  the  impugned  order  the  High  Court  enlarged  the
respondent no.2-Varun Alias Fauji on bail. The respondent no.2
stands trial for offences punishable under Sections 147, 148,
149, 302, 307, 452, 504, 506 and 34 of the Indian Penal Code,
1860 in case Crime No.303/2022 (S.S.T.No.2672/2021)pending on
the file of Sessions Court, Saharanpur, Uttar Pradesh. The
respondent no.2 is accused of causing death of one Monu and
also injury to Sethpal-father of the deceased Monu. The
incident was supposed to happen on 09.08.2022. On an earlier
occasion, when the respondent no.2 was enlarged on bail, the
petitioner approached this Court by filing a Special Leave
Petition (crl.)No.12080/2023. By an order of 01.10.2024, this
Court set aside the order of the High Court granting bail to
respondent no.2 by holding that bail should not have been
granted to respondent no.2 at that stage. This order was made
on 01.10.2024. Thereafter on 12.11.2025, the respondent no.2
moved a fresh bail application before the High Court and the
High  Court  under  the  order  impugned  has  enlarged  the
respondent no.2 on bail.
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner contends that murder
of Monu was in broad day light, by causing fire arm injury and
equally a fire arm injury was caused on the complainant -
father  of  Monu  i.e.  Sethpal.  Learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner further contends that there are 06 antecedents
against  the  respondent  no.2.   Learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner submits that High Court has only mentioned about

--- Page 157 ---
3
respondent no.2 being on bail in the said cases and has
brushed aside the aspect of criminal antecedents. Learned
counsel for the petitioner contends that prima facie no case
of bail is made out to the respondent no.2 and prays for
setting aside the order of the High Court granting bail to
respondent no.2.
4. To the contrary, learned counsel for respondent no.2
submits that as far as the antecedents are concerned, the
cases except the one pertaining to the case Crime No.37/2006
(where the respondent no.2 is exonerated) and case Crime
No.356/2007 the other case are inter-related to the present
case.
5. Be that as it may, the trial is under-way. We have seen
evidence of the witnesses examined. The complainant-Sethpal is
yet to be examined. We are not to be taken to have observed
anything on the merits of the case. These observations are for
the purpose of considering the prayer of the petitioner for
setting aside the bail order.
6. Considering the overall facts and circumstances of the
case  and  keeping  in  mind  the  criminal  antecedents  of
respondent no.2, we are of the opinion that the High Court
erred in enlarging the respondent no.2 on bail. We are
inclined to set aside the order of the High Court granting
bail to the respondent no.2. Accordingly, the prayer of the
petitioner is allowed.
7. We hereby set aside the impugned order passed by the High
Court.

--- Page 158 ---
4
8. The respondent no.2 shall surrender within a period of 10
days from today.
9. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the above
terms.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed
of.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                                  (KOMAL)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                       COURT MASTER(NSH)
*appearance not given

--- Page 159 ---
 IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL  NO.        OF 2026
(@ Special Leave Petition (Crl.) No.4605/2025)
JASPAL SINGH WALIA          ...APPELLANT(S) 
                                VERSUS
HARPREET CHAWLA & ORS.                   ….RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
2. We have heard learned counsel for the respective
parties in the presence of the appellant and respondent
no.1.
3.  Learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  appellant  and
respondent no.1 submitted that despite opportunity being
given to both the parties to have the company of the
child on every Sunday between 11.00 A.M and 3.00 P.M in
Ludhiana, there is severe acrimony between the parties at
the time of respondent no.1 exercising her visitation
rights vis-a vis the minor daughter who is about six
years of age. Therefore, they seek a modification in the
interim arrangement that has been made.
4. It is further brought to our notice that there is a
stay of the proceedings initiated by the appellant herein
seeking dissolution of his marriage. Learned  counsel
1

--- Page 160 ---
for the appellant submitted that the said stay may be
vacated by this Court.
5. In response, learned counsel for the respondent
submitted that liberty may be reserved to respondent no.1
to seek permanent custody of the minor daughter  by
filing an appropriate petition before the Family Court.
6. We take note of these submissions. Therefore, we
firstly, vacate the interim order of stay granted in HMA
No.12719/2024 pending before the Principal Judge (Family
Court),  Ludhiana  wherein  the  appellant  is  seeking
dissolution of his marriage by a decree of divorce vis-a-
vis respondent no.1 herein.
 Secondly,  interim  orders  dated  25.07.2025  and
04.09.2025 are modified by putting in place an interim
arrangement whereby during the summer vacation of the
child,  the  order  dated  04.09.2025  shall  be  made
applicable, i.e.,  from 11.00 A.M to 3.00 P.M. Once the
academic year begins from 08.07.2026,  respondent no.1
shall exercise her visitation rights vis-a-vis the minor
child only on second and fourth Sunday of every month
between 11.00 A.M to 3.00 P.M. All other terms and
conditions of the order dated 04.09.2025 shall continue.
In other words, on the first and third Sunday of every
month, the child shall remain with the appellant-father. 
Thirdly, We also reserve liberty to respondent no.1
herein to seek permanent custody of the child by filing
an appropriate petition before the Family Court. 
2

--- Page 161 ---
7. It is needless to observe that should a petition be
filed by respondent no.1 before the Family Court, the
same shall be decided on its own merits, as expeditiously
as possible in accordance with law. 
8. It is also made clear that the arrangement made in
this appeal is subject to final orders to be made by the
Family Court if a petition is filed by the respondent
seeking permanent custody of the minor child. In other
words, the aforesaid direction is by way of an interim
arrangement.
The parties are also at liberty to seek dissolution
of their marriage by mutual consent. Consequently, the
impugned order is set aside. 
The present Appeal is allowed and disposed of in the
aforesaid terms.

  [B.V. NAGARATHNA]
  ..….……………………………………J.
      [SATISH CHANDRA SHARMA]

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.7840   OF 2026
BANKEY BIHARI GOYAL   ...Appellant(s)
                  Vs.
DURGA DAS AGRAWAL   ...Respondent(s)
         
        
 O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. Anubhav, learned counsel appearing for the
appellant, and Mr. Vipul Ganda, learned counsel appearing
for the sole respondent/Resolution Professional.
2. The impugned order has rejected the claim of the
appellant, a suspended Director for the supply of a copy of
the Resolution Plan of the Successful Resolution Applicant
(SRA).  
3. The  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  appellant
contends that the suspended Director is entitled to a copy
of the plan.  Learned counsel further contends that the case
law relied upon namely, Yashdeep Sharma v. Tara Chand Meenia
Company Appeal (AT) (Insolvency) No.1906 of 2024 is wholly
inapplicable,  since that was a case of the rival applicant
which the appellant is not. Learned counsel also submits
1

--- Page 164 ---
what was originally submitted was a settlement under Section
12-A of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016 and not a
Resolution Plan by the appellant.
4. Mr. Vipul Ganda, learned counsel for the respondent
on the other hand submits that the appellant has suppressed
the fact that he has got the plan.  Apart from that learned
counsel submits that one of the applicants is a related
party of the present appellant.  Learned counsel also drew
attention to Section 12-A application by the appellant.
5. After arguing for 64665some time, learned counsel for
the respondent submits that without prejudice to the rights
and contention, he has no objection to give a copy of the
plan of the SRA, Mr. Varun Chopra to the appellant herein.  
6. In view of the statement of the learned counsel for
the respondent, nothing further survives to be adjudicated
in this appeal.
7. We direct the respondent/Resolution Professional to
furnish a copy of the plan of the SRA within two days from
today.
8. The consequence will be that when the appellant
participates in the proceedings in IA No.29 of 2024 before
the Adjudicating Authority, he will have a copy of the plan
and his objections will be considered in accordance with
law.
2

--- Page 165 ---
9. Now that the issue of supply of a copy of the plan is
sorted out, the National Company Law Tribunal will dispose
of the approval application expeditiously, considering the
fact that the plan is pending approval for the past two
years.
10. With the above observation, the appeal is disposed
of.
..........................J.
       (K.V.VISWANATHAN)
                          
 ..........................J.
       (VIPUL M. PANCHOLI) 
NEW DELHI;
May 26, 2026.

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (C) No. 35616/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 26-09-2025
in FAO(OS)(COMM) No. 239/2023 passed by the High Court of Delhi at
New Delhi]
THE TRUSTEES OF PRINCETON UNIVERSITY               Petitioner(s)

- MODIFICATION OF COURT ORDER AND IA No. 110793/2026 - PERMISSION
TO FILE ADDITIONAL DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES)
 
WITH
SLP(C) No. 1517/2026 (XIV)
Date : 29-05-2026 These matters were called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJAY KUMAR
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Chander Lall, Sr. Adv.
                   Ms. Nancy Roy, Adv.

                   Mr. Manish Sharma, AOR
                   
                   Dr. Aditya Sondhi, Sr. Adv.
                   Mr. Kishor Lambat, Adv.
                   Ms. Suja Joshi, Adv.
                   Mrs. Kashmira Lambat, Adv.
                   Mr. Devika Dhawan, Adv.
                   Mr. Rijuk Sarkar, Adv.
M/s. Lambat & Legiteam, AOR
For Respondent(s) : 
                    Dr. Aditya Sondhi, Sr. Adv.
                   Ms. Suja Joshi, Adv.
                   Mr. Kishor Lambat, Adv.
                   Ms. Kashmira Lambat, Adv.
1

--- Page 168 ---
                   Ms. Devika Dhawan, Adv.
                   Mr. Rijuk Sarkar, Adv.
                  M/s. Lambat & Legiteam, AOR
                   
       UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Having heard the learned senior counsel for the parties at
length and given the peculiar facts of this case, we propose to
dispose of this petition with a few additions to the impugned
order passed by a Division Bench of the Delhi High Court. 
2. As C.S.(Comm) No. 270/2022 is pending consideration before a
learned Judge of the High Court of Delhi, we do not wish to
venture any opinion on the merits of the matter. We may,
however, note that the learned Judge denied interim relief to
the petitioner but the same was reversed in appeal, to some
extent, by a Division Bench of the High Court,  vide  the
impugned order dated 26.09.2025 in FAO(OS)(Comm) No. 239/2023.
3. Thereby, the Division Bench observed that the petitioner had
made out a prima facie case in its favour, but when it came to
the question of irreparable loss and balance of convenience it
opined that, as the respondents have been using the impugned
mark since the year 1991 and their presence was confined only
to the State of Telangana, the petitioner had failed to make
out an adequate case to meet the tests in that regard. The
Division Bench accordingly thought it fit to restrain the
respondents from using the mark ‘Princeton’ and any other mark
deceptively similar thereto for any new institution during the
pendency of the suit, but permitted them to continue to use the
2

--- Page 169 ---
mark for the institutions which were already being run under
the name of ‘Princeton’, subject to the condition that they
file affidavits of the receipts from such institutions, every
six months.
4. There can be no doubt that the petitioner, an Ivy League school
in the United States of America (USA), has its reputation and
goodwill to protect. Students may well be misled by the use of
its name by others offering educational courses. In that view
of  the  matter,  we  deem  it  appropriate  to  add  further
conditions, in addition to what have been imposed by the
Division Bench of the High Court, to safeguard its interest and
also the interests of prospective students of the respondents’
institutes/colleges.
5. The  respondents  shall,  accordingly,  notify  and  publish  a
disclaimer on their Website, their Prospectus(es) and any other
admission  material  that  is  distributed  amongst  candidates
applying for admission to the six institutes/colleges being run
by them under the name ‘Princeton’, stating in clear terms that
they have no connection whatsoever with Princeton University,
New Jersey, United States of America. The disclaimer on the
Website shall be in the largest font size used therein.
6. Apart from the above, the respondents shall also publish a
quarter-page  notice  in  ‘Deccan  Chronicle’  and  ‘Eenadu’
newspapers  having  circulation  in  the  State  of  Telangana,
stating  that  the  six  institutes/colleges  that  they  run,
mentioned clearly by name, have no connection whatsoever with
3

--- Page 170 ---
the Princeton University, New Jersey, United States of America.
Such notices shall be published for a week, on alternate day
basis, that is, on 1st, 3rd, 5th, 8th, 10th and 12th of June, 2026.
7. The respondents shall prominently display a Board outside each
of the six institutes/colleges stating to the same effect,
i.e., that the institute/college has no connection whatsoever
with the Princeton University, New Jersey, United States of
America.
8. Further, in the event the respondents issue any certificate(s)
to  their  students  in  the  name(s)  of  the  six
institutes/colleges, such certificates shall also contain the
aforestated disclaimer in clear and bold letters.
9. The above directions shall be acted upon and given effect to
before the admission process commences for the next academic
year.
10. We make it clear that all issues are left open to be considered
in  C.S.  (Comm)  No.  270/2022.  We,  however,  reiterate  the
observation made by the Division Bench that the parties shall
endeavour to have the suit decided expeditiously. 
11. The special leave petitions are disposed of in the above terms.
Pending applications shall stand disposed of.
(DEEPAK GUGLANI)                             (PREETI SAXENA)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                         COURT MASTER (NSH)
4

--- Page 171 ---

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Our order dated 25.05.2026 reads thus:-
“1. Our order dated 7.5.2026 reads thus:-
“1. Exemption Applications are allowed.
2. Having heard the learned counsel appearing for the
petitioner and having gone through the materials on record,
we are of the view that no case is made out for grant of
bail.
3. The trial shall proceed further expeditiously.
4. We are informed that the victim is missing since long.
5. We are further informed that the victim is mentally
unstable. If the victim is missing, it is the duty of the
State to trace her out at any cost.
6. We want the State to deploy special police force to find
out the whereabouts of the missing girl. Her whereabouts
must be traced within a period of 15 days from today.
7. Post this matter after 15 days to report further status
of the victim.”
2. Today,  when  the  matter  was  taken  up  for  further
hearing, the learned counsel appearing for the State brought
to our notice that in pursuance of the directions issued by
this Court, a Special Investigating Team was constituted, and
the said team has been able to trace out the missing victim
who is mentally unstable.  She has been now kept at the “One
Stop Centre, Faridabad”.  We are informed that it is a shelter
home.
3. We direct the Child Welfare Committee, Faridabad to
ensure that due care is taken of the victim in all respects.
By the next date of hearing, we want an exhaustive report in
the form of an affidavit at the end of the State highlighting
in what manner the victim was traced out, the place where she
was confined and who all are responsible for the trafficking
of the mentally unstable victim.
2

--- Page 181 ---
SLP (Crl.)  No.4589/2026
4. We would also like to have a report from the Child
Welfare Committee, Faridabad, as regards the overall health of
the victim and what further steps should now be taken to
ensure that she lives a better life.
5. List on 27.5.2026.”
2. Today, when the matter was taken up for further hearing,
the learned counsel appearing for the parties informed us that
the victim has gone back to her home.
3. As on date, she is under due care of her parents.
4. However, this is not the end of the matter. We believe that
Police will have to keep a constant vigil and ensure that the
victim is in no manner exploited at any point of time, being
mentally unstable. We order accordingly.
5. Whether  the  Child  Welfare  Committee,  Faridabad  has,
jurisdiction or not, we direct the Child Welfare Committee,
Faridabad to periodically, at least once in 15 days depute two
responsible officers to visit the home where the victim is
residing, talk to her family members and enquire about her
overall health, both mental and physical.
6. There shall not be any lapse on the part of the Police as
well as the Child Welfare Committee, Faridabad.
3

--- Page 182 ---
SLP (Crl.)  No.4589/2026
7. With the aforesaid, we close this matter.
8. One copy of this Order shall be forwarded by the Registry
to the SHO, Police Station Sarai Khawaja, Faridabad, as well as
to the Child Welfare Committee, Faridabad, at the earliest.
9. The  Police  as  well  as  the  Child  Welfare  Committee,
Faridabad, shall ensure that the parents do not ill-treat the
victim in any manner.
10. Let the matter be posted for compliance after 06 months
with the Status Report of the victim from the Police as well as
the Child Welfare Committee, Faridabad.
11. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
(CHANDRESH)                                     (POOJA SHARMA)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION NO.1419 OF 2026 
WITH
IA No.75287/2026 (FOR APPROPRIATE ORDERS/DIRECTIONS)
IN
CIVIL APPEAL NO.14761 OF 2025
DANESH SINGH & ORS              ... APPELLANTS/APPLICANTS
                  VERSUS
HAR PYARI (DEAD) THR. LRS. & ORS.      ... RESPONDENTS
                                                
          O R D E R
1. Upon  being  mentioned  the  aforesaid  Miscellaneous
Application is taken on Board.
2. By our order dated 25.05.2026, we had said that the amount
of Rs.75,00,000/- (Rupees seventy five lakhs) with accrued
interest, if any, shall be disbursed by the Registry in favour
of  the  12  legal  heirs,  after  proper  verification  and
identification in equal share.
1

--- Page 184 ---
3. The learned counsel appearing for the legal heirs brought
to our notice that originally there were 10 legal heirs out of
which one legal heir has passed away leaving behind three
children and that is how the figure 12 has been arrived at.
4. To put it in other words, 09 legal heirs will receive
Rs.7,50,000/- (Rs. Seven lakh fifty thousand only) each along
with accrued interest, if any. The 10 th legal heir who has
passed away leaving behind three children are entitled to
receive Rs.2,50,000/- (Rupees Two lakh fifty thousand only)

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
PETITIONS FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (C)  NOS. 27388-27389/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 21-08-2025
in WPS No.1177/2025 and WPS No.1094/2025 passed by the High Court
of Uttarakhand at Nainital]
NAVEEN SINGH RANA                                  PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF UTTARAKHAND & ORS.                        RESPONDENT(S)
IA No. 228892/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 228889/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 85129/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
IA No. 228886/2025 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 27-05-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Arvind Kumar Shukla, Adv. 
                   Mr. Nihal Ahmad, AoR
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Sanjay Mani Tripathi, Adv.
                   Mr. Mrinal Tripathi, Adv.
                   Ms. Saumya Shikha, Adv.
                   Ms. Anu Gupta, AoR                   
                   
                   Mr. Ayush Anand, AoR
                   Mr. Gaurav Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Monu Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Vishal, Adv.
                   Mr. Shivam Singhania, Adv.
                   Mr. Ritu Raj, Adv.
                   Mr. Krishan Kant Kumar, Adv.
                   Mr. Vanaj Vidyan, Adv.
                   
1

--- Page 192 ---
SLP (C)  NOS. 27388-27389/2025
                   
                             O R D E R
At the outset, we note that the present petition has
been filed by the petitioner, who is said to be the
whistle blower in the case leading to dismissal from
service of the respondent no.5.
2. Learned counsel for the petitioner submitted that
besides challenge to the appointment/engagement of the
respondent  no.5,  the  irregularities  in  respect  of
respondent no.3- Nagar Panchayat was also under question
and upon inquiry, it has been detected that there has been
financial  irregularities  under  the  supervision  of
respondent no.5 and recovery order has also been passed,
but the respondent no.3 has sat over the said order and no
steps have been taken.
3. Be that as it may, we note that the respondent no.3 is
the party which is really aggrieved by the order of
termination being stayed. However, the respondent no.3
not moving in appeal against the order granting stay of
such termination order, in the tentative view of the
Court, is nothing short of collusion of the respondent
no.3 with the private respondent no.5. We further observe
that non-action by the respondent no.3 for recovery of the
2

--- Page 193 ---
SLP (C)  NOS. 27388-27389/2025
Government money also appears to be further indicative of
the respondent no.3’s complicity. At this stage, we have
been informed that the said order of recovery has been
stayed. The Court would observe that it was all the more
reason  that  the  respondent  no.3  should  have  appealed
against the said order of stay when in a fact finding
inquiry it had come to the conclusion that there has been
loss of government revenue. But the respondent no.3 not
moving against the said order of stay, clearly shows
respondent no.3 to be in the dock.
4. Accordingly, we direct the State to take note of these
facts and initiate appropriate proceedings against the
officials/persons of the respondent no.3 for such conduct,
as observed above.
5. We also observe that it is unfortunate that the
respondents no.1 and 2, State of Uttarakhand and its
functionaries are not represented before the Court today.
6. Let respondents no.1 and 2 file a detailed response in
terms of what has been recorded in the order above. Copy
of this order be forwarded to the Chief Secretary, State
of Uttarakhand to submit a reply. We caution that if on
the next date of listing there is no appearance on behalf
of the State of Uttarakhand and no affidavit is filed in
terms of what has been observed in this order, the Court
may initiate appropriate proceedings against the Chief
3

--- Page 194 ---
SLP (C)  NOS. 27388-27389/2025
Secretary, State of Uttarakhand.
7. List on 15.07.2026 at 2.30 p.m. as part heard.
 (POOJA SHARMA)                                (ANJALI PANWAR)
    AR-CUM-PS                               ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
4

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (C) NO.19177/2026:
1. Petitioner is the respondent no.5 in a writ petition 1 under Article 226 of
the  Constitution  filed  by  the  respondent  no.1  before  the  High  Court  of
Uttarakhand at Nainital.
2. An interim order dated 21st May, 2026 has been made by a Division Bench
of  the  High  Court  whereby  allotment  of  a  liquor  license  in  favour  of  the
petitioner has been stayed and an inquiry directed in view of perceived illegality
in the process. 
3. We find from the order impugned that the writ petition is due to be listed
next on 04th June, 2026.
4. Mr.  Amit  Anand  Tiwari,  learned  senior  counsel  appearing  for  the
petitioner, submits that the interim order was obtained by the respondent no.1
from the Division Bench by suppressing material facts as well as by placing on
record incorrect facts.
5. We need not examine the points raised by Mr. Tiwari at this stage having
regard to the fact that the writ petition is due to be listed before the Division
Bench on 04th June, 2026.  
6. We leave it open to the petitioner to raise all points before the High Court
on that date to secure vacation of the impugned interim order.  
7. We hope and trust that the Division Bench will pass an appropriate order
upon hearing all the parties. 
8. The special leave petition stands disposed of.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
1 WPMB No.404 of 2026
2

--- Page 200 ---
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (C) NO.19127/2026:
10. The order passed above while disposing of SLP (C) No.19177/2026 shall
govern this special leave petition too.
11. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
    (MANIK KUMAR)                           (SUDHIR KUMAR SHARMA)

ANUPAM SHARMA           Respondent(s)
O R D E R
This is a petition filed under Section 25 of the Code of
Civil Procedure, 1908 (for short “CPC”). The petitioner is the
wife of the respondent.  
The petitioner has filed this transfer petition seeking
the following reliefs:
“a. Transfer HMA Petition 1512/2024 under section
13(1)of the Hindu Marriage Act 1955,  titled  Sh.
Anupam  Sharma  Vs.  Smt.  Charu  Sharma   pending
adjudication in the court of Ld. Principal Judge,
Family Court, Faridabad, Haryana to the Court of
Ld. Principal Judge, Family Court, Meerut, Uttar
Pradesh;  and 
b. Pass such other order, orders or direction as this
Hon’ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts
and circumstances of the case.”
We have heard learned counsel for the petitioner and
learned counsel for the respondent. 
Having heard learned counsel for the petitioner and
learned counsel for the respondent and on perusal of the
material on record, we find that the petitioner has made out a
case for transfer of the aforesaid petition.
Contd..

--- Page 202 ---
- 2 -  
In the result, the transfer petition is allowed HMA
Petition 1512/2024 pending in the Court of Principal Judge,
Family Court, Faridabad, Haryana is ordered to be transferred to
the Court of Principal Judge, Family Court, Meerut, Uttar
Pradesh;
However, liberty is reserved to the respondent to appear
before the Court in the aforesaid matter either in person or
through video conferencing facility as per the timings of the
Court in Meerut.  If such a request is made, the Court shall
make available video conferencing facility to the respondent
herein.
 Registry to communicate this order to the transferor as
well as the transferee courts for taking steps in accordance
with this order.

CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
   CIVIL APPEAL NO.          OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP (C) No._______ OF 2026)
(Arising out of SLP (C) Diary No.29596/2026)
 A. GURUSWAMI PANDIYAN                    APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
LT. GEN.K. SURENDRANATH PVSM & ORS.      RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Leave granted.
3. The appellant challenges the judgment and order dated
16.12.2025 in RCREV. NO. 151 of 2025 titled “A. Guruswami
Pandyan vs. Lt. Gen. K. Surendranath PVSM and Ors. ” passed
by the High Court of Kerala at Ernakulam.
4. We  have  heard  learned  counsel  for  the  appellant.
Finding  the  Bench  not  to  be  in  agreement  with  the
appellant, the appellant, through learned counsel, seeks
time to hand over the vacant and peaceful possession of the
demised premises to the respondent(s)/landlord(s) on or
before 30.06.2027. Prayer accepted.
5. The appellant shall furnish an undertaking before this
Court to vacate the premises on or before 30.06.2027. The
undertaking, in the usual form, shall be filed by the
1

--- Page 211 ---
appellant within three weeks from today, failing which the
Registry shall take appropriate steps.
6. The  appellant,  through  learned  counsel  present  in
Court, has been made aware of the consequences of breach of
such  undertaking,  including  initiation  of  contempt
proceedings.
7. The arrears of rent due and payable, if any, shall be
positively cleared within three weeks from today.
8. Needless  to  add,  till  such  time  as  the  appellant
occupies  the  premises,  he  shall  continue  to  pay
occupational charges at the same rate at which the rent was
agreed to be paid.
9. Accordingly, the present appeal is disposed of.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

IA No. 120161/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED
JUDGMENT, IA No. 120163/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 29-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Shreeyash Uday Lalit, Adv.
                   Mr. Narendra Kumar Goyal, Adv.
                   Mr. Mukesh Kumar Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Aamir Khan Pathan, Adv.
                   Mr. Jeetendra Kumar, Adv.
                   Ms. Komal Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Kajal Rani, Adv.
                   Mr. Bidya Nand Thakur, Adv.
                   Mr. Bipin Kumar Saxena, Adv.
M/S. Mukesh Kumar Singh And Co., AOR      
             
                   
For Respondent(s)  Ms. Swati Ghildiyal, AOR
                   Mr. Adarsh Dubey, Adv.  
                 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Heard  Mr. Shreeyash Uday Lalit, learned counsel for the
petitioner and  Ms. Swati Ghildiyal, learned counsel for the
State.

--- Page 214 ---
2
2. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that all other
accused persons are on bail.
3. Learned counsel for the State vehemently  opposes the
bail to the petitioner on the ground that on an earlier
occasion, when the petitioner was released on interim bail, he
jumped bail and was absconding for more than two years. This
statement is not disputed by the learned counsel for the
petitioner, whose only contention is that the other accused
persons  are  on  bail  and  that  the  petitioner  cannot  be
perpetually penalized for that past conduct.
4. Learned counsel for the State also submits that though
there are 36 witnesses listed, all are not proposed to be
examined and the list will be pruned. The charges have already
been framed.
5. Under these circumstances, we direct that, if the trial
is not completed in the next 08 months, the petitioner will be
at liberty to renew his prayer for bail. We are saying so, on
the special facts of this case since the petitioner has jumped
the bail.
6. Under these circumstances, liberty to renew the bail
prayer  before  the  Trial  Court  concerned  after  08  months
subject  to  the  conditions  that  the  petitioner  is  not
responsible for delaying the trial.
7. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the above
terms.

--- Page 215 ---
3
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed
of.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                                  (KOMAL)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER(NSH)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.     of 2026
[ARISING OUT OF SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRL.) NO.6344/2026]
AKASH KALIA                                  Appellant(s)
                          VERSUS
STATE OF PUNJAB                              Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 05.03.2026,
passed  by  the  High  Court  of  Punjab  and  Haryana  at
Chandigarh rejecting the anticipatory bail prayer of the
appellant in connection with First Information Report (FIR)
No.181/2025 at P.S. Gate Hakima, Police Commissionerate
District Amritsar.
4. After considering the submission of learned counsel
for the appellant, on 10.04.2026, the following interim
protection order was passed:
“1. Issue notice, returnable in four weeks.
2. In the meantime, it is provided that if
the petitioner is arrested in connection
with  FIR  No.0181/2025,  PS  Gate  Hakima,
District Police Commissionerate, Amritsar,
Punjab & Haryana, he shall be released on
furnishing a personal bond of Rs. 25,000/-
and an undertaking that he shall cooperate
1

--- Page 217 ---
in  the  investigation  and  make  himself
available  for  interrogation  as  and  when
required by the investigating agency. He
shall also submit an undertaking that he
will not threaten the witnesses or tamper
the evidence.”
 
5. On service of notice, the respondent is represented.
6. We have heard learned counsel for the parties.
7. We do not find a good reason to discharge the interim
protection granted earlier.  Consequently, we deem it
appropriate to dispose of the appeal by making the interim
order absolute subject to the following two conditions.
(A) The appellant shall co-operate in the investigation
and make himself available for interrogation as and
when required by the investigating agency.
(B) Within three weeks from today, he shall submit bail
bonds to the satisfaction of the Trial Court concerned
along with an undertaking that he shall not threaten
the witnesses or tamper the evidence.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
……………………………………………………………………………J
                              [MANOJ MISRA]
………………………………………………………………………………J
                              [MANMOHAN]
New Delhi
May 29, 2026

                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
PETITION FOR SPECIAL LEAVE TO APPEAL (CRL.)  NO.  9649/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  16-04-2026
in SBCRMSBA No. 1560/2026 passed by the High Court of Judicature
for Rajasthan at Jaipur]
ARJUN KUMAR PRAJAPATI                              PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF RAJASTHAN                                 RESPONDENT(S)
FOR ADMISSION 
IA No. 158549/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 26-05-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. Namit Saxena, AoR
                   
For Respondent(s) : Ms. Sanskriti Pathak, AAG
                    Mr. Chhatresh Kumar Sahu, Adv. 
                    Ms. Shagufa Khan, Adv. 
                    Mr. Saurabh Rajpal, AoR
                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the petitioner and learned
counsel for the respondent-State of Rajasthan, who has
appeared on advance notice. 
2. The present petition has been filed seeking bail in
connection with FIR No. 174 of 2024, registered at Police
Station Bindayaka, District Jaipur (West), Rajasthan, for
1

--- Page 232 ---
SLP (CRL.)  NO.  9649/2026
the offences punishable under Sections 344, 376(2)(n) and
307 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860, read with Sections
5(1), 5(l) and 6 of the Protection of Children from Sexual
Offences Act, 2012 and Sections 3(1)(w), 3(2)(v) and 3(2)
(va)  of  the  Scheduled  Caste  and  Scheduled  Tribe
(Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989.
3. The allegation against the petitioner is that he had
enticed  the  granddaughter  of  the  complainant  and  was
illegally keeping her and living together as husband and
wife, having physical relations and on the fateful day,
the granddaughter of the complainant had died of injuries
on her head allegedly inflicted by the petitioner.
4. Learned counsel for the petitioner submitted that they
were living together happily and it was a pure accident,
inasmuch as, the deceased had climbed the roof for fixing
the wire of the dish antenna from where she accidentally
slipped and the same resulted in injuries leading to her
death in the hospital after 3 days. Learned counsel for
the petitioner has also drawn the attention of the Court
to the deposition of PW-1/grandfather of the deceased-
complainant, who says that he was informed by one Arjun
Lodhi that the deceased had gone to the roof to fix the
wire of the dish antenna and while fixing the same she
slipped and was then admitted to the hospital and when he,
the PW-1, reached the hospital, his granddaughter who was
2

--- Page 233 ---
SLP (CRL.)  NO.  9649/2026
unconscious, regained consciousness and narrated the same
fact to him and thereafter again she became unconscious.
5. It  was  contended  that  in  such  background,  the
petitioner being in custody for almost 23 months, the
Court may consider releasing him on bail.
6. Learned counsel for the respondent-State of Rajasthan
opposes the prayer for bail.
7. Having considered the matter in its entirety and going
through the material on record, we are not inclined to
allow the prayer for bail, for the present. Accordingly,
the Special Leave Petition stands dismissed.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of. 
 (POOJA SHARMA)                                (ANJALI PANWAR)
    AR-CUM-PS                               ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

                             O R D E R
Heard learned counsel for the petitioners and learned
counsel for the respondents no.1 and 2. 
2. Respondent no.4 is duly served, but there is no
appearance on his behalf. 
3. Learned  counsel  for  the  petitioners  submits  that
respondent no.3 had passed away a couple of days prior to
the filing of the present petition, of which he was
unaware. However, he submits that respondents no.1 and 2
are the heirs of respondent no.3. Therefore, he has filed
IA  Nos.165339/2026  and  165340/2026  for  setting  aside
abatement and to accept the respondents no. 1 and 2 as his
heirs.
4. We note here that the Office has raised objections/
defects in the applications on the ground that the death
certificate of respondent no.3 has not been filed.
5. On a query to learned counsel for respondents no.1 and
2 as to whether their father had died and the date
mentioned in the application(s) is correct, he does not
controvert.  Accordingly,  the  objections/defects  are
waived.
6. Having regard to the aforesaid, prayer for accepting
respondents no.1 and 2 as heirs of respondent no.3 is
allowed at the risk of the petitioners.
2

--- Page 236 ---
SLP (C)  NO.  6055/2026
7. Delay condoned.
8. Abatement is set aside.
9. Cause title be amended accordingly.
10. List on 08.09.2026.
11. Interim order passed by this Court on 04.02.2026, to
continue till the next date of listing.

--- Page 238 ---

2. The petitioner is aggrieved by non-grant of interim
order of stay in his favour in a case before the High
Court where he has filed for quashing of the substantive
FIR itself.
3. Learned  Senior  Counsel  contended  that  the  entire
exercise of him approaching the High Court pursuant to a
statutory right available to him in the matter, is in
effect being frustrated as the matter has not been finally
taken up and disposed of on the one hand, and on the other
hand  there  is  no  interim  protection  given  to  the
petitioner. It was contended that the petitioner has a
good case on merits and, at least, the High Court should
decide the matter finally, one way or the other.
4. Learned  counsel  for  the  respondent  no.1/State  of
Punjab submits that the High Court already seized of the
matter.
5. Learned counsel for the State further submitted that
as the matter relates only to interim stay, the State has
no issues. However, it was submitted that the petitioner
be directed to cooperate in the main case.
6. Learned counsel for the respondent no.2/complainant
submitted that the matter is being heard on regular basis
and has now been posted for 15.07.2026.
7. Having regard to the aforesaid, in our considered
opinion, ends of justice would be served if the interim

--- Page 239 ---

Ludhiana is made absolute till the final disposal of CRM-M
No. 30332/2025 (O&M) by the High Court of Punjab and
Haryana.
8. We make it clear that it shall be open to the High
Court to proceed and decide the substantive case filed by
the petitioner expeditiously. We have been assured by
learned counsel for the parties that the parties shall
cooperate in the said pending proceedings.
9. The Special Leave Petition stands disposed of in the
aforementioned terms.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Learned Solicitor General appears on behalf of the Union of
India. 
2. Pursuant to our directions dated 25.05.2026, the Director
(Legal), National Testing Agency, has filed an affidavit indicating
the  process  by  which  the  recommendations  of  the  High-Level
Committee of Experts were implemented and also the procedure in
conducting the NEET-UG 2026. 
3. Dr. K. Radhakrishnan, Former Chairman of ISRO, who also heads
the High-Powered Steering Committee, has also filed an affidavit
indicating the implementation of the recommendations of the High-
Powered Steering Committee and the future course of action. 
4. We are informed by the Solicitor General that in the memo of
parties,  Union  of  India  is  joined/represented  through  the
Secretary, Ministry of Health and Family Welfare instead of the
Ministry of Education. Let the necessary substitution be carried
out in the memo of parties. 
5. We direct the Union of India to file an affidavit indicating
how and in which manner the process of conducting and concluding
the NEET examination, year after year, would be done. The affidavit
2 of 3

--- Page 245 ---
shall indicate the method by which regulating and supervising
authorities  of  NEET  examination  can  develop  and
retain institutional memory so that they can act on the basis of
the experience, data, and information they have gathered and
processed with each examination cycle.  The endeavour must be to
ensure that the National Testing Agency has the wherewithal,
physical and intellectual, to prevent incidents such as the 2024 or
2026 examinations from occurring in the future. 
6. The affidavit shall be filed within a period of six weeks from
today. 
7. List on 24.07.2026.

ITEM NO.23               COURT NO.15               SECTION II-D
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL)……………………..Diary No. 29173/2026
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated 20-03-2025 in
WPCRL No. 898/2025 05-02-2026 in CRLMA No. 3971/2026 passed by the
High Court of Delhi at New Delhi]
RAMAN KUMAR ARORA                                  Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE GNCT OF DELHI & ORS.                         Respondent(s)
(IA No. 164938/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 164939/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT)
 
Date : 29-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Harikishan, Adv.
                   Mr. Parminder Singh Bhullar, AOR
                                      
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Mr. Harikishan, learned counsel for the petitioner submits
that without notice to the petitioner the High Court has proceeded
on the basis of the submissions made by the private respondent
herein about certain threats allegedly.
3. Learned counsel is particularly aggrieved by paragraph ‘2’ of
the order dated 20.03.2025 which reads as under:
“2. The facts reveal that the petitioners and respondent
no. 5 are cousins involved in a family dispute. The
petitioners  have  been  receiving  threats  from  the

--- Page 253 ---
2
respondent no. 5 and his family for withdrawing all
pending complaints filed against the respondent no.5”
4. We notice that in the order of 05.02.2026 where recall of the
order of 20.03.2025 was sought, the High Court has recorded in para
‘5’ as under:
“5. Be that as it may, considering the nature of the
order dated 20.03.2025, which has been passed to secure
the fundamental right of the petitioners enshrined in
Article 21 of the Constitution of India, coupled with the
fact that the said order was passed without issuance of
notice and/or without going into the merits involved,
there is no reason for this Court to recall the said
order, and that too at this belated stage whence much
water has already flown. Moreover, this Court upon query
has been told that though it was open for respondent no.
5 to take other appropriate remedies qua that and/or
otherwise in accordance with law, undisputedly, have not
been taken till to date.”
5. Since the order of 20.03.2025 was passed without issuing
notice to the petitioner, we clarify that none of the observations
in para 2 made by the High Court in the order of 20.03.2025 are
part of the findings of the High Court. In fact, the same have been
clarified by the High Court in para 5 of the Order of 05.02.2026.
6. Based on the order of 20.03.2025 no adverse inference can be
drawn against the petitioner herein.
7. With the above observations, the Special Leave Petitions are
disposed of.
8. Pending application(s), if any shall stand disposed of.
    (LOKESH ARORA)                                   (KOMAL)
SENIOR PERSONAL ASSISTANT                       COURT MASTER (NSH)

--- Page 254 ---
1

                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. Petitioner  has suffered 21  years  of actual incarceration pursuant to a
conviction recorded by the trial court under Section 302 of the Indian Penal
Code, 1860. 
3. He applied for parole, which was not considered by the authorities. This
resulted in filing of a special criminal application 1 before the High Court. The
same is pending.
1 Special Criminal Application (Direction) No.512 of 2026
1

--- Page 261 ---
4. By filing these special leave petitions, an order is prayed for expediting
the decision on such special criminal application. 
5. We find that the High Court has fixed 25 th June, 2026 for hearing the
special criminal application.

application  upon  hearing  the  parties  on  25 th June,  2026,  or  immediately
thereafter.  
7. If the respondent-State has not yet filed but wishes to file any counter-
affidavit, it may do so by 15 th June, 2026. Rejoinder thereto, if any, may be filed
by the petitioner by 22nd June, 2026.
8. The special leave petitions stand disposed of on the above terms.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The parties sought to explore a mediated settlement which, unfortunately,
has failed.
1

--- Page 295 ---
2. The  application  for  modification  filed  by  the  petitioner-wife  is  now
scheduled to be heard by the Family Court on 4th June, 2026.
3. We have, by an earlier order, stayed the operation of the last sentence of
paragraph 6 of the impugned order dated 21 st April, 2026, till today. Such stay
shall continue till 04th June, 2026, for the present.
4. We leave it open to the Family Court to pass an appropriate order on the
said application for modification including confirming, varying and/or altering
the  order  dated  21 st April,  2026  regarding  visitation  rights  of  the
respondent/husband. Needless to observe, should the order granting visitation
rights be confirmed, the Family Court may make such directions for facilitating
interaction  between  the  respondent/husband  and  the  children  during  their
schedule of summer vacation. Petitioner-wife, if aggrieved by such order and if
so advised, may challenge it in an appropriate proceeding and pendency of this
special leave petition would not create any bar for the relevant court to hear
such challenge.
5. List the special leave petition in the week commencing 27 th July, 2026, for
reporting developments.
6. The Family Court is encouraged to decide the application for modification
on its own merits. If any other connected application is pending, the Family
Court may also proceed to consider and decide the same.
7. All points and contentions on merit are kept open.
(RASHMI DHYANI PANT)                        (SUDHIR KUMAR SHARMA)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)

ITEM NO.65                 COURT NO.9                  SECTION IV-A
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CIVIL) Diary No. 67191/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 01-09-2025
in WA No. 200155/2025 passed by the High Court of Karnataka at
Kalaburagi]
THE STATE OF KARNATAKA & ORS.                      Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
SUNIL KUMAR AGRAWAL                                Respondent(s)
(FOR ADMISSION AND IA No. 8588/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN 
REFILING /  CURING THE DEFECTS)
 
Date : 29-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJAY KUMAR
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) : 
Mr. Aman Panwar, AAG
Mr. Manav Kaushik, Adv.
Ms. Patil Rekha Chandra Gouda, AOR             
For Respondent(s) : 
                   Mr. Tarun Gupta, AOR
                   Mr. Hirday Virdi, Adv.
          UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Delay condoned.
Learned counsel appearing for the State of Karnataka and
others relies upon the photographs filed as Annexure P-7 at pages
36 to 38 of the special leave petition paper book, to assert that
the land in question is a water body.  However, the learned counsel
appearing on caveat for the respondent states that the existing
water body is limited to an extent of 2 acres and the extent of 5
1

--- Page 367 ---
SLP(C) Diary No. 67191/2025
acres purchased by the respondent does not fall within the ambit of
the water body.  
This aspect requires to be examined. Issue notice, returnable
on 24.08.2026.
Notice shall be served by all modes, including dasti.
In the meanwhile, the Principal District and Sessions Judge,
Bidar, Karnataka, is requested to depute a judicial officer not
below the rank of Civil Judge (Senior Division) to undertake an
inspection of the land in question, claimed by the respondent, and
submit a report to this Court as to whether the same forms part of
the existing water body or lies beyond that. The judicial officer
shall give notice to both the parties before undertaking the
inspection. The report shall also indicate as to what is the exact
extent of the water body. Assistance of the Revenue authorities
shall be extended by the district administration in this regard.
Necessary documents shall be placed before the judicial officer by
both sides.  
Registry shall forthwith forward a copy of this order to the
learned Principal District and Sessions Judge, Bidar, Karnataka.
(DEEPAK GUGLANI)                             (PREETI SAXENA)
DEPUTY REGISTRAR                         COURT MASTER (NSH)
2

--- Page 368 ---

                             O R D E R
1. Heard  Ms.  Vrinda  Bhandari,  learned  counsel
appearing for the petitioner.
2. Learned counsel submits that on 10th February, 2026,
the Court had risen due to health exigencies and the
petitioner was under the impression that the matter would
be re-notified.  In fact the matters on the Board which
1

--- Page 373 ---
were not taken up, it was informed by a Notice that it
will be taken up on 11 th February, 2026.  This Notice was
missed  by  the  learned  counsel  for  the  petitioner.
However, it was later transpired that the matter was
dismissed for default on 10th February, 2026.  
3. Learned counsel submits that it was a first default
and the petition may be restored.  Learned counsel also
submits that pending the Writ Petition in the High Court,
a Transfer Petition has also been moved in this Court to
transfer the petitioner’s Writ Petition and to be heard
along with Writ Petition No.161 of 2025 titled Software
Freedom Law Centre, India  v.  Union of India.   In the
Transfer Petition also notice has been issued.

on Mr. Vikramjit Banerjee, learned Additional Solicitor
General.
