Petition for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No. 6796/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 09-03-2026
in MCRC No. 8357/2026 passed by the High Court of Madhya Pradesh at
Gwalior]
SANMAN SINGH GURJAR                                Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH                            Respondent(s)
(FOR ADMISSION  and IA No. 115382/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING
O.T.)
 
Date : 29-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJAY KUMAR
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) Mr. Abhay Gupta, Adv.
                   Mr. Atul Gupta, Adv.
                   Mr. Paramjeet Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Himani Kaur, Adv.
                   Mr. Praharsh Chaudhary, Adv.
                   Mr. Praveen Swarup, AOR
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) Mr. Yashraj Singh Bundela, AOR
                   Mr. Arpit Garg, Adv.
                   Ms. Shrinidhi Gupta, Adv.
                                      
        UPON hearing the counsel, the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
Learned counsel for the State of Madhya Pradesh furnished
details of the past antecedents of the petitioner, Sanman Singh
Gurjar. 
From the statement filed, we find that First Information
Report Nos. 20/2009, 95/2015, 162/2015 and 65/2016 were registered
1

--- Page 383 ---
against the petitioner. However, the petitioner failed to disclose
these facts in the special leave petition.  
In the light of the law laid down by this Court in “Munnesh
vs. State of Uttar Pradesh”1, the failure to disclose these details
is fatal to the petitioner’s case. 
The special leave petition is, accordingly, dismissed on this
short ground. 
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
 (BABITA PANDEY)                                (PREETI SAXENA)
    AR-cum-PS                           COURT MASTER (NSH)
1 2025 SCC OnLine SC 1319

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made
the following
O R D E R
1

--- Page 417 ---
PRAYER OF INTERIM RELIEF IN CRL. A. NO.2342/2026:
The appellant has been convicted by the
Trial  Court  for  the  offences  punishable  under
Sections 302, 324 and 34 of the Indian Pe nal Code,
1860,  and  has  been  sentenced  to  undergo  life
imprisonment. 
The  appeal  preferred  by  the  appellant
challenging the conviction, before the High Court,
came to be dismissed. Aggrieved by the same, he is
before us. 
It is not in dispute that the co-accused has
been granted suspension of sentence. The appellant
has been under incarceration for nearly nine years.
Considering the above, we are inclined to
suspend the sentence of the appellant, and grant
bail to him, during the pendency of the appeal.
Accordingly, the sentence imposed upon the
appellant  stands  suspended,  and  he  is  granted
bail, subject to the terms and conditions that may
be imposed by the trial Court, during the pendency
of the appeal. 
List the appeal in due course.
(SWETA BALODI)                 (POONAM VAID)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS       ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR
2

issuance  of  appropriate  directions  by
this Hon'ble Court in the Special Leave

Petitioner appropriate posting in terms
of the representations;

1

--- Page 423 ---
this  Hon'ble  Court  may  deem  fit  and
proper in the facts and circumstances of
his case and in the interest of Justice.”
2. Delay condoned.
3. Having heard learned counsel for the petitioner and
perused the material available on record, we allow the
petitioner to make a fresh representation, which we are
sure would be considered and decided by the respondents on
an expeditious basis.

         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s) : 
                   Mr. Anurag Dubey, Adv.
                   Mr. Ashish Kumar Upadhyay, Adv.
                   Ms. Maitri Goal, Adv.
                   Mr. Varun Ranjan, Adv.
                   Mr. P. V. Yogeswaran, AOR                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Mr.  Anurag  Dubey,  learned  counsel  for  the
petitioner seeks liberty to withdraw the present Special
Leave Petition.
2. We  also  notice  that  apart  from  the  present
petition, three other Special leave Petitions were filed
1

--- Page 445 ---
challenging the same impugned order.  One petition was
filed under Diary No.37420 of 2023.  This petition was
dismissed for not curing the defects vide order of the
Hon’ble Judge in Chambers on 12 th January, 2024.  Two
other petitions being Diary No.541 of 2024 and 542 of
2024 which are both lying at the stage of defect.
3. In spite of the order of this Court dated 12 th
January, 2024, the defects in these petitions have not
been cured.
4. While permitting Mr. Anurag Dubey, learned counsel
for  the  petitioner  to  withdraw  this  petition  and
dismissing this petition as withdrawn, we direct the
Trial Court to proceed with the trial and conclude the
same expeditiously.  In fact in the order of 12 th January,
2024, this Court had clarified that the pendency of the
Special Leave Petition shall not come in the way of the
Trial  Court  proceeding  further  with  the  trial  in
accordance with law.
5. With  the  above  observation,  the  Special  Leave
Petition is dismissed as withdrawn.
6. The Registry to communicate this order as well as
the order of this Court dated 12 th January, 2024 to the
Court of the Special Judge (SC/ST), Kanpur Nagar, Uttar
Pradesh for appropriate action.
(ANITA MALHOTRA)                             (KOMAL)
   AR-CUM-PS                              COURT MASTER

1

--- Page 449 ---

following terms:

those witnesses who need to be examined, on all
dates, as may be fixed by the Trial Court; and

seek any unnecessary adjournment.

disposed of.

disposed of.

                   Mr. Gaurav Singh, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
We have heard learned counsel for the petitioner.
We  dispose  of  this  Special  Leave  Petition  by
clarifying that the revenue entries are always subject to
the result of the Civil Suit pending between the parties
wherein the validity of the sale would also be considered.
It is needless to observe that the Civil Suit would
be decided based on the evidence let in by the respective
1

--- Page 493 ---
parties and not solely on the basis of the revenue
entries.
We further clarify that on the basis of the decree
to  be  passed  in  the  suit,  the  revenue  entry  shall
accordingly be modified.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

SLP (C) No. 19264/2026
(IA No. 168008/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED

Date : 27-05-2026 These matters were called on for hearing today.

         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE N.V. ANJARIA
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Ashok Panigrahi, Sr. Adv.

    Mr. Ashok Panigrahi, Sr. Adv.

                   Mr. Shubhanshu Mishra, Adv.

                   Dr. Nitya Thakur, Adv.
                   Mr. Abhinav Jha, Adv
                                     
For Respondent(s) : 
          

--- Page 535 ---
2
UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
SLP (C) NO.18567/2026 (ITEM NO.15) AND SLP (C) NO.19264/2026 (ITEM 
NO.64)
1. Mr. Ashok Panigrahi, learned senior counsel appearing for
petitioners,  would  submit  that  pursuant  to  the  Recruitment
Notification  dated  22.01.2011,  the  petitioners,  being  duly
qualified and eligible, participated for appointment as Shikshya
Sahayak in Bhadrak District, Odisha.  Being successful in the
recruitment,  the  petitioners  were  appointed.   However,  their
appointment was later on cancelled on the ground that they had
submitted  an  incorrect  affidavit(s)  of  having  cleared  +2
examination.
2. Mr.  Ashok  Panigrahi  would  further  submit  that  in  the
Recruitment  Notification,  there  was  no  such  requirement  of
submitting any affidavit nor acquisition of +2 qualification, in
addition  to  the  vocational  course  qualification,  is  a
disqualification or ineligibility in the subject recruitment of
Shikshya Sahayak.  It is, thus, argued that cancellation of
petitioners’ appointment is illegal, more so when they were working
successfully since 30.4.2011.
3. Issue notice to the respondent(s), returnable on 9.9.2026.
4. In the meanwhile, the petitioners shall not be removed from
the post of Shikshya Sahayak in Bhadrak District.
5. SLP  (C)  No.19264/2026  to  be  heard  along  with  SLP  (C)
No.18567/2026.
    (LOKESH ARORA)                          (AKSHAY KUMAR BHORIA)
SENIOR PERSONAL ASSISTANT                     COURT MASTER (NSH)

--- Page 536 ---

 RAJENDRA SINGH @ RAJJAN SINGH                      Petitioner(s)

IA No. 76267/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN REFILING /  CURING THE 
DEFECTS
IA No. 76268/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
For Petitioner(s) : M/S. Varun Thakur & Associates, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) : Mr. Surjeet Singh, Adv.
Mr. Harmeet S. Ruprah, AOR
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. The  petitioner,  a  life  convict  has  invoked  the  writ
jurisdiction of this Court under Article 32 of the Constitution and
has prayed for the following reliefs:-
“(a). issue a writ/direction or order commanding the
respondents authorities to consider the petitioner for
remission under the M.P. pre-mature release policy 2025
and;
(b). pass such other or further order/s as this Hon’ble
Court may deem fit in the facts and circumstances of
this case.”

--- Page 545 ---
2
3. It appears prima facie that the petitioner was put to trial in
two different cases and was held guilty in both the cases and has
been sentenced to undergo life imprisonment.
4. There are two orders of conviction with two orders of sentence
of life imprisonment.
5. The petitioner herein has undergone almost more than 16 years
of sentence so far. He wants the State Government to consider his
request  for  remission  of  sentence.  He  has  preferred  a
representation in this regard.
6. We direct the State Government to look into the representation
preferred by the petitioner in accordance with law and more
particularly, having regard to the remission policy prevailing as
on the date of the alleged offence or any other policy which is
favourable to the petitioner and decide the representation within a
period of three months from today.
7. With the aforesaid, the Writ Petition stands disposed of.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
(HARPREET KAUR)                                 (POOJA SHARMA)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner has been denied regular bail by the
High Court in connection with Case Crime No.416 of 2024
registered with Baldeo Police Station, District Mathura,
State of Uttar Pradesh for the offence punishable under
1

--- Page 583 ---
SLP (Crl.) No.4313/2026
Sections 191(2), 191(3), 190, 109, 115(2), 352, 351(2),
333 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 respectively (for
short, “the BNS, 2023”).
2. We are informed by the learned counsel appearing for
the State that in the month of April, the charge came to
be framed and the Trial Court is now to commence with the
recording of the oral evidence. The prosecution intends to
examine 12 witnesses in the course of the trial.
3. Having regard to the nature of the alleged crime and
other attending circumstances, we are not inclined to
order the release of the petitioner on bail at this point
of time.
4. Let the trial proceed expeditiously.
5. The Trial Court shall see to it that the recording of
the oral evidence commences at the earliest and the entire
trial is completed within a period of 06 months from
today.
6. With the aforesaid, the Special Leave Petition stands
disposed of.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.

1. The petitioner before us was put to trial for the
offence punishable under Section 139 of the Negotiable
Instruments Act. She was held guilty for the alleged
offence. She went in appeal before the Sessions Court. The
appeal also came to be dismissed.
2. She  is  now  before  the  High  Court  invoking  the
1

--- Page 589 ---
SLP (Crl.) No.9764/2026
revisional jurisdiction under Section 397 read with 401 of
the CrPC.
3. The High Court seems to have declined to entertain
the revision application on the ground that unless and
until the petitioner surrenders, the revision cannot be
heard on merits. In such circumstances, the petitioner is
before us.
4. The learned counsel appearing for the petitioner has
instructions from her client to make a statement that the
petitioner is ready and willing to deposit the entire
cheque  amount,  i.e.,  Rs.9,30,000/-  (Rupees  Nine  Lakh
Thirty Thousand only) with the Registry of this Court
within a period of eight (08) weeks from today, i.e., on
or before 27.07.2026.
5. If the amount of Rs.9,30,000/- is deposited with the
Registry of this Court, then notice be issued to the
respondent  (original  complainant).  Dasti  service,  in
addition, shall be permitted.
6. Since the petitioner has expressed her inclination to
deposit the amount, no coercive steps be taken against her
till the next date of hearing.
7. List on 13.08.2026.

                             O R D E R
1. The petitioner before us is the widow of the deceased. She is
the original first informant. She wants the bail granted by the
High Court to the respondent no.2 (accused) cancelled.

parties that the trial has already commenced. The recording of the
oral evidence of the petitioner is over. Within a short period of
time, the trial would be completed.

of bail at this point of time, though, we do not approve the line
of reasoning assigned by the Court while exercising its discretion
for the purpose of grant of bail.
4. Let the trial proceed expeditiously.
5. The Trial Court shall see to it that the trial is disposed of
within a period of 06 months from today.
6. The Special Leave Petition is, accordingly, disposed of.

have the parties physically present before him. The place,
time  and  date  is  left  to  the  discretion  of  learned

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1

--- Page 625 ---
SLP(Crl.)  No.6240/2026
1. Our order dated 06.04.2026 reads thus:-
“1. Heard the learned Senior counsel appearing for
the petitioner.
2. Delay condoned.
3. Exemption Applications are allowed.
4. Issue notice, returnable in two weeks.
5.  Mr.  Prashant  Singh,  the  learned  AOR  waives
service of notice for and on behalf of the State.
6. In the meantime, no coercive steps shall be taken
against the petitioner.”
2. We are informed that even after this Court granted protection
from any coercive steps being taken against the petitioner he has
not cooperated in the investigation till this date.
3. In view of the aforesaid, the Special Leave Petition stands
dismissed.
4. Pending application(s), if any, stands disposed of.

--- Page 631 ---

the learned counsel for the respondent.

notwithstanding the subsequent charge-sheet filed. Unfortunately,
the charge-sheet is not before us. In such view of the matter, we
are not inclined to interfere with the impugned order except by
giving liberty to the petitioner to challenge the charge-sheet
before the High Court, in which case, the impugned order will not
stand in the way.

accordingly.
